analogy: Think of "Metals and Non-Metals" through the chapter's own opening images: Metals and Non-metals 3 CHAPTER In Class IX you have learnt about various elements. You have seen that elements can be classified as metals or non-metals on the basis of their properties. n Think of some uses of metals and non-metals in your daily life.... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Metals and Non-Metals is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that metals and Non-metals 3 CHAPTER In Class IX you have learnt about various elements.

The text explains that you have seen that elements can be classified as metals or non-metals on the basis of their properties. n Think of some uses of metals and non-metals in your daily life. n What properties did you think of while categorising elements as metals or non-metals? n How are these properties related to the uses of these elements?

The text explains that let us look at some of these properties in detail.

The text explains that 3.1 PHYSIC 3.1 PHYSIC 3.1 PHYSIC 3.1 PHYSIC 3.1 PHYSICAL PROPERTIES AL PROPERTIES AL PROPERTIES AL PROPERTIES AL PROPERTIES 3.1.1 Metals The easiest way to start grouping substances is by comparing their physical properties.

The text explains that let us study this with the help of the following activities.

The text explains that for performing Activities 3.1 to 3.6, collect the samples of following metals – iron, copper, aluminium, magnesium, sodium, lead, zinc and any other metal that is easily available.

The text explains that note the appearance of each sample. n Clean the surface of each sample by rubbing them with sand paper and note their appearance again.

The text explains that metals, in their pure state, have a shining surface.

The text explains that try to cut these metals with a sharp knife and note your observations. n Hold a piece of sodium metal with a pair of tongs.

The text explains that cAUTION: Always handle sodium metal with care.

The text explains that dry it by pressing between the folds of a filter paper. n Put it on a watch-glass and try to cut it with a knife. n What do you observe?

The text explains that science 38 You will find that some metals can be beaten into thin sheets.

The text explains that did you know that gold and silver are the most malleable metals?

The text explains that what do you observe? n Repeat with other metals. n Record the change in the shape of these metals.

The text explains that you will find that metals are generally hard.

The text explains that the ability of metals to be drawn into thin wires is called ductility.

The text explains that for example – (i) All metals except mercury exist as solids at room temperature.

The text explains that give an example of a metal which (i) is a liquid at room temperature.

The text explains that at ordinary temperature, the surfaces of metals such as magnesium, aluminium, zinc, lead, etc., are covered with a thin layer of oxide.

The text explains that silver and gold do not react with oxygen even at high temperatures.

The text explains that cAUTION: Do not take sodium and potassium as they react vigorously even with cold water. n Put the samples separately in test tubes containing dilute hydrochloric acid. n Suspend thermometers in the test tubes, so that their bulbs are dipped in the acid. n Observe the rate of formation of bubbles carefully. n Which metals reacted vigorously with dilute hydrochloric acid? n With which metal did you record the highest temperature? n Arrange the metals in the decreasing order of reactivity with dilute acids.

The text explains that in the case of copper, no bubbles were seen and the temperature also remained unchanged.

The text explains that 3.3.1 Properties of Ionic Compounds To learn about the properties of ionic compounds, let us perform the following Activity: Activity 3.13 Activity 3.13 Activity 3.13 Activity 3.13 Activity 3.13 n Take samples of sodium chloride, potassium iodide, barium chloride or any other salt from the science laboratory. n What is the physical state of these salts? n Take a small amount of a sample on a metal spatula and heat directly on the flame (Fig.

The text explains that these compounds are generally brittle and break into pieces when pressure is applied.

The text explains that 4 OCCURRENCE OF MET OCCURRENCE OF MET OCCURRENCE OF MET OCCURRENCE OF MET OCCURRENCE OF METALS ALS ALS ALS ALS The earth’s crust is the major source of metals.

The text explains that the elements or compounds, which occur naturally in the earth’s crust, are known as minerals.

The text explains that at some places, minerals contain a very high percentage of a particular metal and the metal can be profitably extracted from it.

The text explains that some metals are found in the earth’s crust in the free state.

The text explains that they are found in the earth’s crust mainly as oxides, sulphides or carbonates.

The text explains that they are solids at room temperature, except mercury which is a liquid. n Metals can form positive ions by losing electrons to non-metals. n Metals combine with oxygen to form basic oxides.

The text explains that you must have seen tarnished copper vessels being cleaned with lemon or tamarind juice.

The text explains that explain why these sour substances are effective in cleaning the vessels.

The text explains that differentiate between metal and non-metal on the basis of their chemical properties.

The text explains that a man went door to door posing as a goldsmith.

The text explains that he promised to bring back the glitter of old and dull gold ornaments.

The text explains that an unsuspecting lady gave a set of gold bangles to him which he dipped in a particular solution.

The text explains that the bangles sparkled like new but their weight was reduced drastically.

The text explains that the lady was upset but after a futile argument the man beat a hasty retreat.

The text explains that can you play the detective to find out the nature of the solution he had used?

The text explains that give reasons why copper is used to make hot water tanks and not steel (an alloy of iron).

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 3: Metals and Non-Metals

4Al + 3O2 → 2Al2O3 (Aluminium) (Aluminium oxide) Recall from Chapter 2, how copper oxide reacts with hydrochloric acid.

prelims_traps: Do not treat 3 CHAPTER and 3.1.1 Metals as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Metals and Non-Metals; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 3 CHAPTER and 3.1.1 Metals to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
